section: Effects of Cryogenic Rolling on the Microstructure Evolution and Mechanical Properties of Low-Density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C Steel

context_update.summary: The current chunk covers the abstract and introduction of a study on cryogenic rolling (CR) of low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel. It highlights the motivation: low yield strength of austenitic low-density steel due to the FCC structure and extensive slip systems, and the limitations of room-temperature cold rolling because of dynamic recovery promoted by high Al and C contents. The advantages of cryogenic rolling are summarized—reduced deformation resistance and suppressed dynamic recovery enhancing strain hardening. Several prior related works are cited, including Mishra et al. (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.9C, 50% room-temperature rolling achieving 1.5 GPa UTS and 10% elongation), Ren et al. (Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.3C, 90% cold rolling raising yield strength from 570 MPa to 2 GPa), and CR applications in Cu-35Zn, Ti, Al-Mg alloys, and austenitic stainless steel. The abstract previews key results: microstructural evolution from equiaxed austenite to elongated grains with strain bands, twins, dislocations, and texture change from brass to copper; mechanical property ranges with increasing CR—UTS 882→1861 MPa, YS 505→1791 MPa, microhardness 284→578 HV, elongation 64%→6.6%. The next chunk is expected to describe experimental procedures and deformation levels.
mechanisms: dynamic recovery suppression by cryogenic temperature
increased SFE by Al and C promoting cross-slip and climb
dislocation strengthening and grain refinement

context_update.summary: This chunk finishes the introduction, summarizing the benefits of cryogenic rolling (reduced deformation resistance, suppressed dynamic recovery) and prior works on Cu-35Zn, Ti, Al-Mg alloys and austenitic stainless steel. It states that CR effects on low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steel are unreported. The experimental section details the material: Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel with composition C 0.97, Mn 27.91, Al 8.04 (Table 1) and density 6.82 g/cm³. Processing includes homogenization at 1200 °C/2 h, hot forging, solution treatment at 1000 °C/1 h and water quenching, followed by CR with LN₂ immersion, ~2% per pass to cumulative reductions of 30%, 50%, 70% and 90%. Characterization methods: OM, SEM, EBSD, TEM, XRD, microhardness (500 g, 15 s) and tensile testing (dog-bone 20×5×1 mm, 0.2 mm/min). Microstructure evolution (Figures 2-3) shows grain elongation, slip bands, Taylor lattices, dislocation entanglements/cells, deformation twins and nanoscale grains (58.8 nm at 90% CR). XRD (Figure 4) confirms single-phase austenite, FWHM broadening from 0.496° to 1.022°, and increases in microstrain (2.8e-3 to 7e-3) and dislocation density (3.2e14 to 2.36e15 m⁻²). EBSD (Figures 5-7) shows HAGBs/LAGBs evolution, texture transition Cu {112}<111>→brass {110}<112>, and KAM distributions. The next chunk is expected to present mechanical properties and further analysis.
processing_route: cryogenic rolling with liquid nitrogen immersion, ~2%/pass, cumulative reductions 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%
microstructure: undeformed: equiaxed austenite, annealing twins, dislocations
30% CR: elongated grains, slip bands, Taylor lattices
50% CR: flattened/banded grains, dislocation entanglement/walls, abundant deformation twins
70% CR: larger dislocation cells, reduced twin density
90% CR: fibrous grains, micro-shear bands, nanoscale grains (58.8 nm), random orientation, decreased twins
single-phase austenite persists throughout, no martensitic transformation
increased local misorientation and KAM in rolling bands at 90%
mechanisms: cryogenic temperature suppresses dynamic recovery, enhances strain hardening
dislocation accumulation: Taylor lattice → entanglements → walls → cells
deformation twinning active at intermediate strains, then decreases
severe plastic deformation refines grains to nanoscale (58.8 nm)
fibrous morphology promotes interface shear slip
texture change reflects evolving slip/twinning activities

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the EBSD analysis (Figures 5–7) for undeformed, CR 30%, and CR 90% conditions, revealing grain boundary type transitions (undeformed 94.65% HAGBs, 60° twin peak; CR 30% 89.4% LAGBs, copper texture; CR 90% 94.1% LAGBs, brass texture and fibrous morphology with interface shear slip). KAM maps show deformation concentration near grain boundaries at 30% and increased average misorientation with higher deformation. Mechanical properties from Figure 8 show initial UTS 882 MPa, YS 505 MPa, 284 HV, 64% elongation; at 90% CR, values reach 1.86 GPa UTS, 1.79 GPa YS, 578 HV, and 6.6% elongation, demonstrating a strength–ductility trade-off. Fracture morphology (Figure 9) transitions from ductile dimples to ductile-brittle mixed mode with partial cleavage at 90% CR. The discussion attributes deformation mechanisms: plane slip and Taylor lattice at 30% (SRO/κ-carbides), transition to dislocation walls and twinning at 50% (hardening and back stress), dislocation slip dominance at 70% because Hall–Petch effect makes twinning more difficult, and subgrain boundary formation at 90% leading to nanoscale grain refinement. SFE values are 72 mJ/m² (RT) and 51 mJ/m² (cryogenic). Strengthening arises from dynamic slip band refinement, Taylor dislocation hardening, and Hall–Petch grain refinement. The low elongation of heavily rolled material is explained by dislocation saturation and grain boundary absorption of dislocations in nanocrystals. The next chunk may continue with conclusions or additional analysis.
processing_route: solution treatment + cryogenic rolling to reductions of 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%
properties: fracture mode: ductile dimples → partial cleavage (ductile-brittle mixed)
mechanisms: plane slip and Taylor lattice (low strain)
slip band hardening, back stress, twin activation (moderate strain)
SFE decrease at cryogenic temperature
Hall-Petch for slip vs twinning (k_T > k_s)
dislocation tangles → subgrain boundaries (high strain)
dynamic slip band refinement strengthening
Taylor dislocation hardening (forest hardening, Orowan loops)
grain boundary Hall-Petch strengthening
dislocation saturation and grain boundary absorption limiting elongation
figures_or_tables: Figure 5 (IPF and pole figures for undeformed, CR30%, CR90%)
Figure 8 (engineering stress–strain curves and strength/ductility/hardness data)
unresolved_terms: exact role of SRO clusters vs κ-carbides in planar slip under cryogenic conditions

context_update.summary: The current chunk discusses the role of strain hardening rate and strain rate sensitivity in promoting uniform deformation and postponing necking. It explains that a high initial dislocation density after severe deformation can saturate and limit further accumulation, resulting in a low strain hardening rate. In nanocrystalline grains, dislocations easily traverse and are absorbed by grain boundaries, reducing intragrain dislocation storage and restricting the strain-hardening capacity. The combined dislocation and grain boundary strengthening enhances strength and hardness, but the increasing dislocation density and grain refinement gradually decrease elongation. The conclusion states that during CR, twinning dominates at moderate strains, while dislocation slip dominates at low and high strains, driven by grain refinement, SFE, dislocation density, and temperature. The microstructure after CR achieves UTS 1.86 GPa and YS 1.79 GPa, attributed to dislocation strengthening and grain refinement. The strength–ductility trade-off is highlighted. Key variables include strain level, deformation temperature, SFE, dislocation density, and grain size. No new figures or tables are introduced.
mechanisms: dislocation saturation limits strain hardening
dislocation absorption by nanocrystalline grain boundaries
dislocation strengthening hindering motion and increasing flow stress
grain refinement introducing obstacles
twinning-dominated at moderate strains, dislocation slip-dominated at low/high strains
